findings.problem: Sanitization drift weakens the chapter's core reckoning. The marriage gets diagnosed in polished abstractions and category labels instead of being forced through one or two humiliatingly concrete remembered incidents. The sex line at 269 in particular summarizes the damage as a managerial concept rather than rendering bodily or emotional consequence, so the scene stays intelligent but too safe at the exact point where the style bible and constitution call for embarrassing specificity.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:253-312 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:373-391 so at least part of the confession runs through concrete memory instead of thesis language. Keep the blackout kitchen as present-tense frame, but let each spouse anchor an admission to a specific prior domestic or intimate moment: a softened fight, a task replacing fear, a sexual encounter reduced to courtesy, a concrete household failure Nita preempted. Preserve the current emotional conclusion, but make the insight emerge from recalled scene detail, interruption, and embarrassment rather than clean summary.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised material in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:253-312 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:373-391 contains at least two concrete remembered incidents with observable object, sensory, or bodily detail, and at least one of those incidents makes the couple's intimate estrangement legible on the page rather than summarized as a category. Fail if the exchange can still make the same point using only abstractions such as usefulness, supervision, reliability, or action items.

findings.problem: Ben and Nita's voices partially converge in the confession scene. Both speakers start delivering balanced, essay-ready self-diagnoses in the same polished cadence, which weakens the style-bible requirement for idiolect separation. Ben should sound more hedging, smoothing, and self-protective before he snaps to a blunt admission; Nita should sound more operational, cutting, and concretely specific. Here, several adjacent admissions could plausibly belong to either speaker with only minor wording changes.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the admissions in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:253-377 so the same revelations arrive through character-specific rhythm. Give Ben more tonal pivots, hedges, or failed softening before his blunt lines land, and give Nita sharper operational nouns, domestic specifics, and more abrupt cutoffs instead of symmetrical self-analysis. Keep the content of the reckoning, but make the sentence music and pressure unmistakably speaker-specific.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:253-377, Ben's two main admissions each include a smoothing, hedging, or joke-adjacent turn before the hard truth lands, while Nita's two main admissions each hinge on concrete household or systems detail rather than abstract labels alone. Fail if the revised admissions still read as back-to-back polished thesis statements in a shared register.

findings.problem: Leela's reply "Tonight it's." is clipped past natural spoken texture and reads like stylized compression rather than a nine-year-old's literal object logic. That line brushes the constitution's ban on elliptical clipping and briefly breaks the otherwise strong realism of her voice.
findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the exchange at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:159-161 with a grammatically complete line that still sounds like Leela: literal, object-based, and mildly stubborn. Preserve the beat that she temporarily reclassifies the kitchen on her own terms, but do it without depending on omitted predicate logic for effect.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised reply in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:159-161 is a complete spoken sentence that clearly states Leela's argument about the room and still sounds child-scaled. Fail if the line still relies on an omitted predicate or complement to create terseness.

findings.problem: A negative-definition sentence formula becomes a noticeable chapter tic. Variants of "It was not... It was..." and "There was no..." recur well beyond the allowed two uses, especially in high-pressure emotional beats, which starts to flatten the prose into one preferred rhetorical move instead of letting each moment find its own shape.
findings.rewrite_direction: Vary the sentence architecture in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:39-40, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:223, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:281, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:355, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:399, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:431. Keep the semantic meaning, but recast most of these beats into affirmative image, action, or sensory syntax so the chapter is not repeatedly defining feeling by negation.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised chapter keeps no more than two negative-definition constructions of this exact family across the cited spans and at least three of the current instances are rewritten into affirmative, image-led, or action-led syntax. Fail if the chapter still repeats the same negation-first rhetorical move more than twice.

summary: The chapter gets the blackout's altered soundscape, Leela's child-scaled pressure, and the non-reconciliatory end beat right, but it still fails review because the central marital reckoning leans too hard on polished summary and sanitized abstraction, one Leela line slips into clipped stylization, and a repeated negative-definition sentence shape becomes a prose tic.
